text.title: Logging Patterns Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the logging-patterns methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: All application logs are JSON objects with ts + level + event + request_id + fields. No printf-style text in production code.
rule.rationale: Structured logs are queryable by field; text logs require regex archaeology at 3am.

rule.statement: Every log line within a request carries the same request_id (UUID/ULID), injected by middleware on entry.
rule.rationale: Without correlation, multi-service or multi-worker traces are impossible to reconstruct.

rule.statement: PII (email, phone, card-like) is redacted at the formatter layer, not at call sites.
rule.rationale: Call-site redaction always misses a path; formatter redaction is centralised and auditable.

rule.statement: Levels follow: debug=dev only; info=normal flow; warn=recoverable; error=unhandled; critical=human intervention required.
rule.rationale: Without level discipline, dashboards either flood (everything warn) or miss real issues (everything info).

rule.statement: Request/job egress logs include duration_ms + db_query_count + cache_hit_count.
rule.rationale: These three fields catch the majority of latency regressions without enabling a tracer.

rule.statement: Each service has a logs-per-second budget. Logging in tight loops without sampling is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Unbounded logging is the silent killer of log sinks and ingest budgets.

rule.statement: One-shot script, existing structured logs, or no sink — skip this methodology.
rule.rationale: Logging baseline pays back only with production runtime + a sink to query.
